item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait…how can the instances being documented or not have any effect on when they disappear? It says they vanish 90 days after inception or documentation, whichever comes first. Unless the Foundation is employing time travel to write documentation of an event that hasn't occurred yet, the inception is always first.

Edit: oops, misread.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Per Licensing Staff consensus, image is considered user generated and is released under CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please quit editing the article to change between amnesiacs and amnestics. I want it to be amnestics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, this clicked with me. The anartist behind the anomaly is in some sort of canny peak of pretentious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really use more incidences added on. The last one was documented in 2012 and it's 2015!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that "talented" works as a neutral description of the anartist's abilities, personally. You don't have to approve of their work to think that the entity or group behind 1019 has abilities that makes AWCY? look like a bunch of clumsy, adolescent tryhards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot, and upvoted, but I did find the phrase "talented anartist" to be a bit jarring.
Obviously, many researchers and agents are, as some have said, "genre savvy," but it seems like too much respect for a "criminal" to go into the record. Not harming people, doesn't, in the Foundation's eyes, make something harmless. Each instance of anartism risks the protective illusions so carefully maintained by the Foundation. "Talented anartist" sounds insufficiently disapproving.
Not sure what to replace it with…maybe just remove talented? Or substitute the footnote?
Still a nice piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one it too, unclear for me. Maybe I've been reading too many and my eyes are bugging out, but I had trouble following this and the list just added to my confusion. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. Quite entertaining. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chaos undivided? What are you doing here?
I think its alright. Ill upvote it, but it isn't great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of an SCP being multiple, varying instances as opposed to a single consistent object/entity/event. The little notes are what really secured my upvote, especially the last one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Makes me think of a socially-conscious, teenage Wondertainment. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like that, a self-confirming scp.
They were unrelated, so it would have been wrong to classify them as one anomaly. But because the defining factor is belief that they are related, having the erroneus impression that a given thing is 1019 would make it, retro-actively, correct to file it as such.
It's a beautiful ouroborus of a concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I concur. I think that I wouldn't have upvoted if it wasn't for people forgetting about Waffle House.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1019 is the belief that unrelated extranormal phenomena are instances of an SCP object designated 1019.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if there is no 1019?
Hear me out. There is this list of phenomena, but by definition they can't be verified; because they stopped working when they went on the list. So what the Foundation actually has here is a list of things that some staff member told them a story about. A story that, conveniently and by definition, can not be independently verified.

I think this is a bit like my favorite interpretation of Nobody, a useful tool and/or joke that the underlings use against the administration.

-Did I ever tell you about the time I was out fishing and caught a 30 foot trout that spoke Latin?
-Oh, yeah? Where's the proof? Why didn't you stuff it and hang it on the wall?
-Weren't you listening It spoke latin, I'm not going to just up and murder a man of God just because he happens to be a fish! I let him go, dinnit I? But it was in the local paper, here's a clipping.
-Huh, "Local Man Tells Tale of Talking Trout", I guess that proves it then. I won't doubt you again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This should be your theme song".
Lolwat
I think even in that instance, the Foundation would realize that someone knows a bit too much about them, although I like the messages. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the whole "Waffle House" thing. Those things are like the dandelions of the South.

…I wish I could forget them…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the rewrite! Much better :3 *changes downvote to upvote*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I saw that, I wondered if it was a Foundation employee causing the SCP. They might even have access to the tools.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, well, in my headcanon, at least, anartists aren't really a "group" any more than regular artists are; they're just mostly-unaffiliated people who happen to be out there making art that's anomalous, and their existence as a larger non-AWCY? demographic is kind of implied by AWCY?'s existence. But, yeah, the whole "knows about the Foundation's existence" thing is kinda jarring. Although I guess rumors get around the greater paranormal community in-universe? I dunno. *shrug*

Although I am one of those people who likes the idea of anartists, and of course There Is No Canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. Also, the last entry with the Starbucks solidified my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I'm sold. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this SCP. It was the last thing in the table that made it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP just made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I go with the author's intent if it is provided. And apparently there's a guy who not only can access the Foundation's files, but the Foundation is too stupid to realize they've been compromised in this instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't ruin it, just puts another view out there. When have you been told that it is necessary to use the Author's canon to understand something? I had a view that is neither of these ideas, and hearing other views does not change my opinion of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? Then lost part of it's charm. I was of the idea that the effect itself 'knew' when it was documented, and that was part of the development. If it's 'manually' ended 'cause they read files and find it documented… eh, not so interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never said it was the only, just that it was good to see one~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, this is good now. Just a guy who knows magic, trying to express himself and maybe make some people think. I also like it when the Foundation isn't genre blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those times where revealing the intent behind the article ruins it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the fact that they can't document it if they want to research it, which is cool beyond the obvious annoyance factor. I've headcanoned the idea that info on the current instance spreads through the Foundation's rumor mill.
- "Did you hear about the new ten-nineteen? I heard that it makes people float upside down just off the floor!"
- "No! You can't email that to Stan, word of mouth only, remember?"
- "Is it true that Kevin and Rosetta were caught in the new one-oh-one-nine event? But High Command won't announce it for two more months?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great 682!
You've actually fixed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better then last time. I personaly think it is much better to catalougue all the objects as a whole, as long as no more related articles appear. We already have AWCY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone has their own headcanon. That's what I had in mind when I wrote it, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…seconding Bright.

"talented anartist" kind of kills everything for me. The whole anart thing in general, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took it more as that it was part of the anomalous behaviour that it vanishes when documented, not that the anartist actually has access to the files.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because they disappear when documented, which means that the creator has access to SCP Foundation files, and because the creator is capable of generating anomalous objects and events with many different effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, just by tweaking it a little, it's been vastly improved. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately I did not have the opportunity to read the original, but I think this is an interesting concept. It's like the Banksy of SCPs. But I still have difficulty understanding how loosely affiliated non-hazardous anomalous events are a Euclid-class containment situation and not just a series of anomalous events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They may not be in tune with how the preternatural community moves, but you can bet your ass that they know all of the symbols, definitions, and terminology there is to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This almost feels like this has gone too far in the other direction, and it clashes with my headcannon a bit. I don't think I realized before now, but I've come to take for granted the notion that the Foundation doesn't "get" the preternatural community at large, and something about this article implies a savvy I found jarring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is kind of dumb, yet I kind of like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job on editing this article- much better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno, this still seems to be pushing the definition of what an SCP is. It still reads as a list of anomalous phenomena that's being perpetrated by a single individual or group.

It's like cataloging an author's entire written work as a single book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the changes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh. I dunno. Yet another group that can create SCP like events, and knows of the existance of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My prank SCP isn't really AWCY?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. I liked the first writeup, but this is a definite improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see a Prank SCP that isn't AWCY

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this has been greatly improved from initial posting, well done.
